papers.title: The Binary Fraction of B-type Runaway Stars from LAMOST DR8
papers.author_names: Kun Chen Yanjun Guo Dengkai Jiang Zhanwen Han Xuefei Chen

abstract.text: Runaway stars are defined as stars that depart from their birth clusters at high peculiar velocities. There are two main mechanisms for the formation of runaway stars, i.e., the binary-supernova scenario (BSS) and the dynamical ejection scenario (DES). Investigating the binary fraction of runaway stars is an important step in further exploring the relative significance of the two mechanisms. We analyzed the binary fraction of 203 Galactic B-type runaway stars identified in the Large Sky Area Multi-Object Fiber Spectroscopic Telescope Data Release 8 database. Our analysis of radial velocity variations in the runaway star sample reveals an observed spectroscopic binary fraction of $5.4\\\\%\\\\pm 1.6\\\\%$, representing the proportion of objects that exhibit statistically significant variations in radial velocity with amplitudes larger than $\\\\rm 16~km~s^{-1}$. We employed a Monte Carlo method to correct for observational biases and determined an intrinsic binary fraction of $27\\\\%\\\\pm 8\\\\%$. The period and mass ratio distributions that best reproduce the observation are $f(P)\\\\propto P^{-5.7}$ for $1\\\\leq P\\\\leq 1000$ days, and $f(q)\\\\propto q^{-3.6}$ for $0.1\\\\leq q\\\\leq 1.0$, indicating a preference for binaries with shorter periods and less massive companions compared to a uniform distribution. The intrinsic binary fraction, in combination with previous studies on the binary fractions of runaway stars formed by the BSS and the DES, implies that both scenarios contribute comparably to the formation of Galactic B-type runaway stars, where the ratio of the BSS to the DES is 0.86.

brief.tldr: [astronomical observational study with statistical analysis] The paper analyzes the binary fraction of 203 Galactic B-type runaway stars from LAMOST DR8, finding a spectroscopic binary fraction of 5.4% ± 1.6% and correcting for observational biases to an intrinsic binary fraction of 27% ± 8%. The period and mass ratio distributions favor short-period, low-mass-ratio binaries (f(P) ∝ P⁻⁵·⁷, f(q) ∝ q⁻³·⁶), and combined with prior BSS/DES models, implies a BSS-to-DES ratio of 0.86—suggesting both mechanisms contribute comparably to B-type runaway star formation, with BSS being slightly dominant.

source_fields.title: The Binary Fraction of B-type Runaway Stars from LAMOST DR8

source_fields.sections_text: Conclusion: The intrinsic binary fraction of Galactic B-type runaway stars is 27% ± 8%, indicating a strong influence of binary evolution and dynamical interactions in their formation, with binary disruption playing a major role and the BSS and DES mechanisms contributing similarly to the runaway star population. Discussion: The intrinsic binary fraction of B-type runaway stars is 27%, indicating that the BSS and DES contribute comparably to runaway star formation (with a BSS/DES ratio of X = 0.86), and the lack of detected HMXBs suggests the presence of undetected or quiescent binary systems. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel analysis of early-type runaway stars by combining kinematic and binary fraction data to distinguish between binary-supernova and dynamical ejection formation scenarios, addressing the long-standing problem of explaining the low binary fraction and high velocities of these stars. Multiplicity Analysis: The multiplicity analysis identifies spectroscopic binaries in a sample of B-type runaway stars using a combination of a significance threshold and a minimum RV amplitude criterion, with a final threshold of 16 km s⁻¹ determined by a kink in the binary fraction versus amplitude plot, resulting in an observed binary fraction of 11 out of 203 stars. Runaway Star Sample And Rv Data: This section presents a sample of 203 B-type Galactic runaway stars identified from LAMOST DR8 data, with radial velocity measurements and observational statistics used for subsequent binarity analysis. Intrinsic Multiplicity Properties: This section describes a Monte Carlo simulation method to correct for observational biases and constrain the intrinsic binary fraction and orbital parameter distributions in runaway stars by comparing simulated and observed binary properties using a global merit function based on Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests and binomial probabilities.

abstract: Runaway stars are defined as stars that depart from their birth clusters at high peculiar velocities.
There are two main mechanisms for the formation of runaway stars, i.e., the binary-supernova scenario (BSS) and the dynamical ejection scenario (DES).
Investigating the binary fraction of runaway stars is an important step in further exploring the relative significance of the two mechanisms.
We analyzed the binary fraction of 203 Galactic B-type runaway stars identified in the Large Sky Area Multi-Object Fiber Spectroscopic Telescope Data Release 8 database.
of Galactic B-type runaway stars, where the ratio of the BSS to the DES is 0.86.
title: The Binary Fraction of B-type Runaway Stars from LAMOST DR8

papers.title: Ancient, eclipsing, tidally-locked: A blue lurker progenitor in the population of extreme-velocity star candidates
papers.author_names: A. Bhat M. Dorsch S. Geier U. Heber H. Dawson F. Mattig D. Benitez-Palacios Pablo Fernandez-Schlosser

abstract.text: Many extreme velocity candidate stars have been found based on \\\\textit{Gaia} astrometry, but need spectroscopic confirmation. We select late-type hypervelocity star (HVS) candidates from the \\\\textit{Gaia} DR3 catalog with a $1\\\\sigma$ lower limit of the tangential velocity of 800 km\\\\,s$^{-1}$. J1903-0023, one of the brightest targets, stands out as high priority candidate for follow-up spectroscopy using the X-shooter instrument at ESO-VLT. We determine its atmospheric parameters and abundances utilizing synthetic spectral grids and a global $\\\\chi^2-$minimization procedure, and its stellar parameters with the help of evolutionary tracks and the spectral energy distribution. The star shows variability in its light curve and follow-up spectroscopy confirms that the star is radial-velocity variable. The spectroscopic distance of J1903-0023 is lower than that based on the parallax, indicating that the star is not a hypervelocity binary star but bound to the Galaxy. The star turned out to be of spectral type F, very similar to the extreme-velocity star J0725-2351, which we analyse in the same way as the target. Apparently, both stars are very metal poor and old halo main-sequence (sdF) stars with masses slightly below the halo turn-off mass, and share the low metallicity ([Fe/H]=-2.3,-2.6) and strong alpha enhancement ([$\\\\alpha$/Fe]$\\\\sim0.44$). While J0725-2351 is non-rotating ($v\\\\sin\\\\,i<3$\\\\,km\\\\,s$^{-1}$), J1903-0023 is a fast rotator ($v\\\\sin i=42.3\\\\pm2.0$ km\\\\,s$^{-1}$). The Gaia, and ZTF light curves show an eclipse at a 1.179 day period, similar to the rotation period of J1903-0023. We therefore conclude that J1903-0023 is a high-velocity tidally-synchronised binary most likely with a metal-poor M dwarf companion.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research letter] A high-velocity halo F-type star (J1903-0023) with tangential velocity ~800 km/s is identified as a tidally-synchronised binary with an M-dwarf companion, evidenced by a 1.179-day eclipse in ZTF light curves and radial velocity variability. Spectroscopic analysis reveals low metallicity ([Fe/H] = −2.3), strong alpha enhancement ([α/Fe] ≈ 0.44), and fast rotation (v sin i = 42.3 km/s), consistent with a pre-blue lurker system undergoing mass transfer. Comparison with the non-rotating twin J0725-2351 shows shared halo origin and metal-poor, old main-sequence properties, suggesting that extreme-velocity candidates may not be unbound but instead represent dynamically evolved binaries in the Galactic halo.

source_fields.title: Ancient, eclipsing, tidally-locked: A blue lurker progenitor in the population of extreme-velocity star candidates

source_fields.sections_text: Appendix#5: The origin of J1903-0023 is ambiguous—its trajectory and high $r_{\\mathrm{max}}$ suggest accretion, yet its surface composition resembles in-situ halo stars, while J0725-2351's high $[\\alpha/\\mathrm{Fe}]$ and $L_z$-energy position indicate an in-situ origin despite proximity to merger streams. Conclusion: The study identifies J1903-0023 as a fast-rotating, tightly bound halo binary with a short orbital period, likely spun up by tidal interaction with an M dwarf companion, while contrasting it with the non-rotating, halo-orbiting J0725-2351, and suggests J1903-0023's unusual trajectory and rotation originate from a Galactic stream. Kinematics: This section demonstrates that both J0725-2351 and J1903-0023 are halo stars with distinct, highly eccentric orbits consistent with their retrograde motion and extreme velocities, with J1903-0023 possibly originating from the Phlegeton stream or Sequoia merger. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of identifying a tidally synchronised binary halo star system, likely a pre-blue lurker, among extreme-velocity halo candidates, addressing the problem of distinguishing between ejection mechanisms and binary populations in metal-poor halo stars through precise spectroscopic and photometric analysis. Evolutionary status: The system J1903-0023 is a rapidly rotating, old binary with a synchronized orbit, likely forming via mass transfer that will lead to the M dwarf companion evolving into a blue lurker or blue straggler within the next 1–3 Gyr, while J0725-2351 is confirmed as an ancient halo main-sequence star with no rotation. Light Curve Analysis: J1903-0023 is an eclipsing binary with a 1.179-day period, featuring a cool, low-luminosity secondary star (Teff ≈ 3940 K, R ≈ 0.46 R⊙) inferred from inverse modeling of the light curve, with key parameters constrained by phase-folded analysis and MCMC sampling. Spectroscopic analysis: Spectroscopic analysis reveals that both stars are very metal-poor, old population II subdwarfs with similar chemical compositions, but J1903-0023 is unusually fast-rotating (v sin i = 42 km/s) and shows a radial velocity shift consistent with an eclipsing binary, while J0725-2351 is non-rotating, suggesting a discrepancy in the Gaia parallax that undermines radius and mass estimates. Radii, luminosities, and distances: By combining spectroscopic and photometric data, the study derives accurate radii, luminosities, and distances for J1903-0023 and J0725-2351, revealing significant discrepancies in the distance to J1903-0023 between spectroscopic and Gaia parallax measurements that challenge the reliability of the latter. Stellar masses and ages from evolutionary tracks: Stellar masses and ages are derived using Bayesian MCMC analysis of MIST evolutionary tracks, with results showing consistent masses for J0725-2351 and significant uncertainties for the sdF star, suggesting it may be either very young or old with no definitive single-star solution. Target selection, spectroscopic and photometric observations: This section identifies J1903-0023 as a high-velocity main sequence star with HVS kinematics, estimates its properties from photometry and spectroscopy, and highlights its status as a top-priority candidate with a mass of ~1.7 M⊙ and a radius of 1.5 ± 0.2 R⊙, while noting variability and the need for further radial velocity measurements.

papers.title: The origin of the most recently ejected OB runaway star from the R136 cluster
papers.author_names: Simon Portegies Zwart Michel Stoop Lex Kaper Alex de Kooter Steven Rieder Tomer Shenar

abstract.text: The $\\\\sim 60\\\\,000$ solar-mass (\\\\MSun) star-cluster R136 (NGC~2070) in the Tarantula Nebula in the Large Magellanic Cloud is the host of at least 55 massive stars ($M \\\\apgt 10$\\\\,\\\\MSun) which move away from the cluster at projected velocities $\\\\gteq 27.5$\\\\,km/s \\\\cite{2024Natur.634..809S}. The origin of the high velocities of such runaway stars have been debated since the 1960s, resulting either from dynamical ejections \\\\citep{1961BAN....15..265B,1961BAN....15..291B} or from supernova explosions \\\\citep{1983ApJ...267..322H}. Due to the Gaia satellite's outstanding precision, we can now retrace the most recently ejected binary star, Mel 34, back to the center of R136 and reconstruct the events that 52\\\\,000 years ago let to its removal from R136, i.e., we establish its dynamical interaction and ejection history. We find that this ejection requires the participation of 5 stars in a strong interaction between a triple composed of the tight massive binary Mel~39 orbited by the star VFTS~590, and the binary star Mel~34. The participation of 5 stars is unexpected because runaway stars were not expected to result from triple interactions \\\\cite{2011Sci...334.1380F}. The deterministic nature of the Newtonian dynamics in the scattering enables us to reconstruct the encounter that ejected Mel~34. We then predict that Mel~39 is a binary star with an 80\\\\,\\\\Msun\\\\, companion star that orbits within $\\\\sim 1^\\\\circ$ in the same plane as Mel~34, and escapes the cluster with a velocity of $\\\\sim 64$\\\\,km/s. The five stars will undergo supernova explosions in the coming 5\\\\,Myr at a distance of $\\\\sim 180$\\\\,pc to $\\\\sim 332$\\\\,pc from their birth location (R\\\\,136). The resulting black hole binaries, however, are not expected to merge within a Hubble time.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: runaway stars, unbound, escaping, or ejected stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] A forensic dynamical reconstruction reveals that the ejection of the massive binary Mel 34 from the R136 cluster 52 kyr ago required a five-star encounter involving a tight triple system (Mel 39–VFTS 590 binary) and the binary Mel 34, defying prior expectations that runaways arise from binary supernovae or two-body interactions. The interaction's deterministic Newtonian dynamics allow precise backtracking of velocities and trajectories, predicting that Mel 39 (an 80 M⊙ binary) will escape at ~64 km/s, while all five stars will explode as supernovae in 5 Myr, forming black hole binaries that are unlikely to merge within a Hubble time. This work establishes a rare case of multi-body dynamical ejection in a dense stellar environment, with implications for runaway star formation and cluster evolution.

source_fields.title: The origin of the most recently ejected OB runaway star from the R136 cluster

source_fields.sections_text: Results: A hierarchical triple-binary encounter 52,000 years ago reproduced observed orbital separations, velocities, and eccentricities, with Mel 39 showing a final semi-major axis of 1.39 ± 0.57 au, eccentricity of 0.84 ± 0.10, and a mean runaway velocity of 70.6 ± 8.6 km/s, while Mel 34 retained a semi-major axis of 6.03 ± 9.14 au and eccentricity of 0.74 ± 0.08, and the two binaries were ejected in nearly opposite directions with a median angle of 172°. Appendix#2: The core of a stellar cluster undergoes gravothermal collapse, leading to a top-heavy mass function with a mean mass of ~11 M⊙ in the core and a high stellar density, resembling the observed properties of clusters like R136. Appendix#3: This section demonstrates that Mel 34, a binary in the cluster core, can eject massive stars through dynamical encounters by liberating sufficient kinetic energy—up to ~3000 kT—over its orbital evolution, with each ejection also imparting recoil to the binary itself. Appendix#4: The ejection of VFTS 590 and Mel 34 in the R136 encounter is most likely due to a 4-body scattering event involving a low-mass undetected star, with high collision probabilities ruling out clean ejections and favoring a scenario where collisions are more probable than high-velocity runaways. Appendix#5: This section details the use of expanded SIGMA3 and SIGMA packages to systematically calculate multi-body cross-sections for binary-star interactions, concluding that neither 3-body, 4-body, nor 5-body scattering scenarios can plausibly explain the observed velocities and orbital properties of VFTS 590 and Mel 34. Appendix#6: The evolution of the ejected binaries Mel 34 and Mel 39, modeled using the SeBa code, shows a similar path from main-sequence stars to black hole formation via Roche-lobe overflow and mass transfer, with key phases marked in the shorthand notation. Discussion: The experiment shows that two massive binary systems ejected all observed runaways above 10 M⊙ within the last 0.84 Myr, with half likely from a cold sub-cluster merger, and predicts ~52 additional low-mass runaways; the binaries will evolve into black-hole mergers in ~5 Myr, demonstrating that runaway star observations can reconstruct the dynamical history of young massive clusters. Introduction: This paper presents a novel forensic reconstruction of the dynamical encounter that ejected the fast-moving runaway binary Mel 34 from R136, identifying the participating stars and confirming a violent multi-body interaction as the origin of its ejection—solving the problem of how such high-velocity runaways can form in a young cluster too early for supernova-driven ejections. Reconstructing The Interaction: By conserving energy and momentum, the interaction dynamics of the triple-binary encounter are constrained to a manageable parameter space, with configuration G emerging as the most likely scenario for ejecting VFTS 590. The Ballistics Of The Last Runaways: The ejection of Mel 34 and VFTS 590 requires an unusually energetic encounter, inconsistent with standard binary exchange interactions, suggesting a rare or collisional dynamical event rather than a typical encounter between a bulky binary and a single star. The Last Massive Runaway Star Mel 34: The binary runaway star Mel 34, with masses of ~139 and ~127 solar masses and a high-eccentricity orbit, was ejected from the R136 cluster ~52 kyr ago at ~46 km/s and is consistent with dynamical formation through a binary-binary encounter. The Other Stars Participating In The Encounter: The encounter involving R136's binaries Mel 34 and Mel 39 suggests a mass-conserving ejection event, with a 90 ± 3 M⊙ runaway star (likely VFTS 590's companion) and a 220 M⊙ binary system (Mel 39) ejected at high velocities, consistent with observed masses and orbital parameters.
